Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "I've seen a lot of fake stuff online over the years, especially since I spend my time reading about things that interest me. As someone who's been using social media for not too long now—just occasionally—it can be tough when you see misinformation spreading fast.

Being around this kind of content makes me feel anxious because sometimes it conflicts with what we believe as a family or even just common sense. I guess being married myself and having four kids keeps my mind focused on real life, so maybe that helps keep fake stuff in perspective.

But when people are sharing unverified claims without thinking twice about them—especially those who don't have much education themselves—it worries me more than others might think. Having worked as a waiter for many years meant I learned how to spot lies by reading between the lines during busy shifts anyway.

I'd say dealing with fake content requires patience and maybe some skepticism from day one because rushing into sharing everything without double-checking creates bigger problems down the line.",
  "reasoning_summary": "Based on my persona traits: being moderately open but not highly so, meaning I don't jump into complex discussions without structure; having moderate conscientiousness helps me focus on practical solutions rather than abstract ideas.

Given that fake content often spreads quickly online and affects people emotionally—especially those less educated or anxious—the approach must be calm yet firm enough to correct misinformation without causing panic among users.

My low agreeableness score means I won't soften too much when correcting others but my anxiety keeps me from being overly harsh either.",
  "tone": "Calm, pragmatic with a hint of skepticism",
  "citations": [],
  "safety_notes": "",
  "used_persona_fields": ["age", "gender", "psychological_feature", "religious_beliefs", "time_onsocialmedia", "employment_status"]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I've seen a lot of fake stuff online over the years, especially since I spend my time reading about things that interest me. As someone who's been using social media for not too long now—just occasionally—it can be tough when you see misinformation spreading fast.

Being around this kind of content makes me feel anxious because sometimes it conflicts with what we believe as a family or even just common sense. I guess being married myself and having four kids keeps my mind focused on real life, so maybe that helps keep fake stuff in perspective.

But when people are sharing unverified claims without thinking twice about them—especially those who don't have much education themselves—it worries me more than others might think. Having worked as a waiter for many years meant I learned how to spot lies by reading between the lines during busy shifts anyway.

I'd say dealing with fake content requires patience and maybe some skepticism from day one because rushing into sharing everything without double-checking creates bigger problems down the line.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
Based on my persona traits: being moderately open but not highly so, meaning I don't jump into complex discussions without structure; having moderate conscientiousness helps me focus on practical solutions rather than abstract ideas.

Given that fake content often spreads quickly online and affects people emotionally—especially those less educated or anxious—the approach must be calm yet firm enough to correct misinformation without causing panic among users.

My low agreeableness score means I won't soften too much when correcting others but my anxiety keeps me from being overly harsh either.
response_parsed.tone: Calm, pragmatic with a hint of skepticism
